text: The surgeon prepares for a laparoscopic polyneomectomy by placing three trocars, including one between the umbilicus and pubis. The large subserous myoma, measuring fifteen centimeters, is visible. To reduce the risk of perioperative hemorrhage, the surgeon opens the peritoneum between the round ligament anteriorly and the infraldibilopedic ligament posteriorly to control the uterine arteries at their origin.

The surgeon uses gas to assist in dissection, progressing deeper into the retroperitoneal area to locate anatomical landmarks such as the ureter medially and the external iliac vein laterally. The dissection follows an avascular plane between the uterine and external iliac vein, aiming to find the internal iliac artery. The surgeon performs hemostasis and increases the peritoneal section for better visualization of the retroperitoneal space. The internal iliac artery is exposed, and the uterine artery is identified and clipped at its origin using a ten millimeter clip. The superficial uterine vein is visible during this process.

The surgeon pushes the large myoma to the left, freeing the right retroperitoneal area. The right ureter is clearly visible, and a lateral approach is taken to access the right uterine artery. The peritoneum is opened between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament, allowing a better view from the umbilicus. The medial aspect of the external iliac vein is followed, and the ureter is tracked medially. Precise hemostasis is performed to maintain a clear view of the avascular retroperitoneal space. The peritoneum is cut more cranially, revealing the pararectal fossa and the internal iliac artery, which divides into the umbilical and uterine arteries. A 10mm clip is applied to the uterine artery at its origin, devascularizing the uterus by more than ninety percent. The surgeon coagulates the large vein surrounding the base of the 15cm myoma to reduce perioperative bleeding before beginning the myomectomy.

The surgeon performs a bloodless dissection by opening the uterine cervix and taking small bites of the myometrium, cutting the coagulated serosa with bipolar coagulation. The large forceps are used to tract the myoma cranially, allowing for its removal from the myoma bed. The myoma is placed in the right hypochondrium, and a Vicryl-I stitch with a 14mm needle is used for closing the myoma bed, invaginating surrounding tissue for anatomical reconstruction and hemostasis. The assistants help invaginate the tissue, achieving significant hemostasis with intracorporeal suturing. The surgeon then proceeds to remove a second, smaller subserous myoma using a longitudinal hystereotomy.

The surgeon makes a longitudinal incision in the myometrium to reach the capsule, facilitating the removal of the myoma. The assistant holds the healthy myometrium while the surgeon applies traction to the myoma, allowing for quick extraction. Energy is consistently applied to the myoma, and the removed myoma is placed in the deblash bones. The surgeon uses a large needle to take deep bites of tissue, three centimeters outside the uterine edges, employing intracorporeal knotting to control hemostasis and achieve anatomical reconstruction. The assistant holds the edges of the hysterectomy as the knot is locked and completed using a Vicryl stitch.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal knotting to secure the final stitch in the myoma bed, ensuring a tight closure and good anatomical reconstruction. The surrounding tissue shows signs of ischemia, indicating effective knot tying. The assistant uses forceps to stabilize the tissue during the knotting process. The operative field remains dry with minimal bleeding, demonstrating a successful closure of the myoma bed.

The surgeon begins morcellation of the posterior myoma using an electrical morcellator positioned in the left iliac fossa, operating at 700 volts per minute. Care is taken to avoid spreading myoma pieces inside the abdomen. After approximately ten minutes, the morcellation is completed. The surgeon uses a straight needle to close the 15mm trocar site to reduce the risk of incisional hernia. The pelvic area is inspected and cleaned, confirming no active bleeding and ensuring both clips remain securely in place.
